tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.
  • interp(void*, int, int, int, void*, int, int, int, int): 1 occurrences<>
  • psinv(void*, void*, int, int, int, double*, int): 1 occurrences<>
  • resid(void*, void*, void*, int, int, int, double*, int): 1 occurrences<>
  • unknown: 3 occurrences<>
hint.title: CALL instructions

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
  • IDIV: 1 occurrences<>
  • PUSH: 1 occurrences<>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround: Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 26.00 cycles. At this rate:
  • 5% of peak load performance is reached (9.69 out of 192.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 2% of peak store performance is reached (3.54 out of 128.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 26.00 to 17.00 cycles (1.53x speedup).

0% of peak computational performance is used (0.00 out of 48.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
• Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:
    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA): for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized function can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your function is not vectorized.
Only 16% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 26.00 to 3.44 cycles (7.56x speedup).

gain.workaround:
• Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:
    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA): for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized function can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your function is not vectorized.
Only 16% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 26.00 to 3.44 cycles (7.56x speedup).
